content.body: Semiconductors have electrical conductivity between conductors and insulators. Silicon and germanium are common semiconductors used in electronic devices.

Semiconductor types:
n-type: doped with group 5 element (e.g. phosphorus) → extra free electrons (negative carriers)
p-type: doped with group 3 element (e.g. boron) → 'holes' (positive carriers)

p-n junction diode:
Forward bias: p connected to +, n to − → current flows
Reverse bias: p connected to −, n to + → no current (depletion layer)

content.title: Diode Applications
content.body:
Rectification: converting AC to DC
Half-wave rectifier: single diode — passes only positive half-cycles
Full-wave rectifier: 4 diodes (bridge) — converts both half-cycles to positive
Smoothing: capacitor added to smooth DC output

LED (Light Emitting Diode): emits light when forward biased

content.title: Transistors
content.body:
BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor) as switch:
Base current controls Collector-Emitter current
Small base current → large collector current (amplification)
If base current sufficient → transistor saturates (switch ON)

MOSFET: voltage-controlled switch
Used in logic gates and microprocessors

questions.question: A semiconductor has conductivity
questions.explanation: Semiconductors conduct less than metals but more than insulators, and conductivity can be controlled by doping.

questions.question: n-type semiconductor is made by adding
questions.explanation: Group 5 atoms have 5 valence electrons. In silicon lattice (4 bonds needed), 1 extra electron is free to conduct.

questions.question: A p-n junction diode allows current when
questions.explanation: Forward bias: positive terminal pushes holes (p-side) and negative pushes electrons (n-side) toward junction → current flows.

questions.question: Rectification converts
questions.explanation: Rectification: diodes pass current in one direction only, converting AC (alternating) to DC (direct/unidirectional) current.

questions.question: A capacitor in a rectifier circuit is used for
questions.explanation: Capacitor charges during peaks and discharges between peaks, filling in the gaps to give smoother DC.

questions.question: An LED emits light because
options: electrons and holes recombine at the junction releasing photons
questions.answer: electrons and holes recombine at the junction releasing photons
questions.explanation: In an LED, recombining electrons and holes release energy as photons of light. Different materials give different colours.

questions.question: In a transistor used as a switch, a small base current
options: controls a much larger collector-emitter current
questions.answer: controls a much larger collector-emitter current
questions.explanation: BJT amplification: small input (base) current controls large output (collector-emitter) current — the basis of switching and amplification.

questions.question: Logic gates use
options: digital signals (0 and 1) to perform logical operations
questions.answer: digital signals (0 and 1) to perform logical operations
questions.explanation: Logic gates are digital circuits that produce outputs based on Boolean logic — inputs are either HIGH (1) or LOW (0).

questions.question: AND gate output is 1 only when
questions.explanation: AND gate: output = 1 if and only if input A AND input B are both 1.

questions.question: Microprocessors are made from millions of
questions.explanation: Modern microprocessors contain billions of MOSFET transistors etched onto a silicon wafer, acting as switches for binary logic.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Digital electronics uses binary (two-state) signals. Logic gates perform Boolean operations on binary inputs to produce outputs.

Truth tables for basic gates:
AND: A·B, output=1 only if A=1 AND B=1
OR: A+B, output=1 if A=1 OR B=1 (or both)
NOT: Ā, output = inverse of input
NAND: NOT(AND) — opposite of AND
NOR: NOT(OR) — opposite of OR
XOR: output=1 if inputs are DIFFERENT

content.title: Logic Circuits
content.body:
NAND and NOR are universal gates — can make any other gate

Adder circuit: adds binary numbers
Flip-flop: memory element — stores one bit
Combinational logic: output depends only on current inputs
Sequential logic: output depends on inputs AND previous state (memory)

content.title: Binary Numbers
content.body:
Decimal to binary:
13 = 8+4+1 = 1101₂

Binary to decimal:
1011₂ = 8+0+2+1 = 11

Byte = 8 bits
1 KB = 1024 bytes, 1 MB = 1024 KB

questions.question: AND gate output is 1 when
questions.explanation: AND: output=1 only if A AND B are both 1. If either is 0, output=0.

questions.question: OR gate output is 0 when
questions.explanation: OR gate: output=0 only if BOTH inputs are 0. Output=1 if any input is 1.

questions.question: NOT gate with input=1 gives output
questions.explanation: NOT gate inverts: input 1 → output 0. Input 0 → output 1.

questions.question: NAND gate is equivalent to
questions.explanation: NAND = NOT(AND). It is the inverse of an AND gate.

questions.question: XOR gate output is 1 when
questions.explanation: XOR (exclusive OR): output=1 if inputs differ. 0+1=1, 1+0=1, but 0+0=0 and 1+1=0.

questions.question: 1101₂ in decimal
Decimal 10 in binary
NAND gate is called universal because
options: any logic function can be built from NAND gates only
questions.answer: any logic function can be built from NAND gates only
questions.explanation: NAND (and NOR) are universal: NOT, AND, OR, XOR can all be constructed from multiple NAND gates.

questions.question: A flip-flop in digital electronics
options: stores one bit of information (memory element)
questions.answer: stores one bit of information (memory element)
questions.explanation: Flip-flop: bistable circuit storing one binary digit. Basis of computer memory (RAM, registers).

questions.question: How many values can 4 bits represent?
questions.explanation: 4 bits: 2⁴ = 16 combinations (0000 to 1111).

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

lessons.title: Cathode Ray Tube and Electron Beams

content.body: The cathode ray tube (CRT) uses a beam of electrons to produce images. It demonstrates important properties of electrons and electric/magnetic fields.

In CRT:
1. Electron gun: cathode heated → thermionic emission
2. Electrons accelerated by anode (V) → gain KE
   eV = ½mv² → v = √(2eV/m)
3. Deflected by electric/magnetic fields
4. Phosphor screen glows where electrons hit

Magnetic deflection: F = Bev (Biot-Savart)
Electric deflection: F = Ee = eV/d

content.title: Applications and Oscilloscope
content.body:
Oscilloscope (CRO): displays electrical signals
X-plates: deflect beam horizontally (time axis)
Y-plates: deflect beam vertically (signal voltage)
Time base: sweeps beam across screen at known rate
Measure: period, frequency, amplitude of signals

content.title: Thermionic Emission
content.body:
When metal is heated to high temperature:
• Electrons gain enough energy to escape the surface
• Tungsten cathode used (high melting point)
• Basis of vacuum tubes, X-ray tubes, electron microscopes

Photoelectric emission: light ejects electrons (Einstein, 1905)
• hf > work function φ to eject electron

questions.question: Thermionic emission is
options: electrons emitted from hot metal surface
questions.answer: electrons emitted from hot metal surface
questions.explanation: Thermionic emission: heating a metal gives surface electrons enough energy to escape. Used in CRT electron guns.

questions.question: In a CRT, electrons are accelerated by
questions.explanation: High voltage applied between cathode and anode accelerates electrons: eV = ½mv² gives them kinetic energy.

questions.question: Energy gained by electron accelerated through voltage V
questions.explanation: Work done on charge e through potential V: W = eV (in joules, where e=1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C).

questions.question: Magnetic force on moving electron is
options: perpendicular to both velocity and field
questions.answer: perpendicular to both velocity and field
questions.explanation: F = Bev. Force is perpendicular to both v and B — causes circular motion, not acceleration.

questions.question: In an oscilloscope, the Y-plates control
questions.explanation: Y-plates: signal applied here deflects beam vertically proportional to voltage — shows signal amplitude.

questions.question: Oscilloscope measures frequency by
options: measuring period T from screen then f=1/T
questions.answer: measuring period T from screen then f=1/T
questions.explanation: One cycle visible on screen. Period T = (divisions per cycle) × (time base setting). f = 1/T.

questions.question: CRT has been largely replaced in TVs/monitors by
questions.explanation: LED/LCD flat screens are thinner, lighter, use less power, and have better resolution than bulky CRT displays.

questions.question: X-rays are produced by
options: accelerating electrons and hitting them on heavy metal target (anode)
questions.answer: accelerating electrons and hitting them on heavy metal target (anode)
questions.explanation: X-ray tube: fast electrons decelerate suddenly in metal anode → high-energy X-ray photons emitted (bremsstrahlung).

questions.question: Phosphorescent screen in CRT glows because
options: electrons transfer energy to phosphor atoms which emit visible light
questions.answer: electrons transfer energy to phosphor atoms which emit visible light
questions.explanation: Electrons hit phosphor, exciting electrons to higher energy levels. They fall back, emitting visible photons.

questions.question: Electron microscope uses electrons instead of light because
options: electrons have much shorter wavelength → can resolve much smaller details
questions.answer: electrons have much shorter wavelength → can resolve much smaller details
questions.explanation: Resolution limited by wavelength. Electrons (de Broglie wavelength ~0.004nm) resolve 100,000× better than visible light.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Electronics. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Electronics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Electronics builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Electronics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Electronics. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Electronics include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Electronics. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
